title: Molar Mass
subtitle: Connecting moles to mass
subject: Chemistry
syllabusDotPoints: Calculate molar mass from chemical formulae
Use molar mass to connect mass and amount of substance
lessonIntent: Students can calculate molar mass and use it to move between grams and moles.
examSkill: Identify molar mass as the bridge between mass and moles in quantitative chemistry questions.

scenes.caption: Molar Mass: connecting moles to mass.

voiceover.text: Molar Mass. Connecting moles to mass.

scenes.heading: Particles have mass
scenes.body: The mole tells us how many particles we have. Molar mass tells us how much one mole of those particles weighs.
scenes.callout: Counting is useful when we can weigh it.
scenes.caption: The mole counts particles. Molar mass tells us the mass of one mole.

voiceover.text: The mole tells us how many particles we have. Molar mass tells us how much one mole of those particles weighs.

scenes.heading: What is molar mass?
scenes.body: Molar mass is the mass of one mole of a substance, measured in grams per mole.
scenes.callout: One mole has a predictable mass.
scenes.caption: Molar mass is the mass of one mole of a substance.

voiceover.text: Molar mass is the mass of one mole of a substance, measured in grams per mole.

scenes.heading: Units matter
scenes.body: Molar mass is written in g mol⁻¹.
scenes.secondary: It links the atomic scale to a measurable mass in the lab.
scenes.callout: Read g mol⁻¹ as grams per mole.
scenes.caption: Molar mass is written in grams per mole, or g mol to the negative one.

voiceover.text: Molar mass is written in grams per mole. It links the atomic scale to a measurable mass in the lab.

scenes.heading: Mass, moles and molar mass
scenes.secondary: m is mass in grams, n is amount in moles, and M is molar mass in grams per mole.
scenes.caption: Use m equals n times M to calculate mass from moles and molar mass.

voiceover.text: Mass equals amount in moles multiplied by molar mass.

scenes.heading: Worked example
scenes.question: Calculate the mass of 2.00 mol of carbon dioxide, CO₂.
scenes.coachNote: Target: mass. So use m = n × M.
scenes.caption: Calculate molar mass first, then multiply by the amount in moles.

voiceover.text: Calculate the molar mass of carbon dioxide first, then multiply by two point zero zero moles.

scenes.heading: Common mistake
scenes.body: Do not confuse molar mass with mass.
scenes.secondary: Molar mass is the mass of one mole. Mass is the amount of substance you actually have.
scenes.callout: M is a rate. m is the sample mass.

diagram.beforeContent: The mass of exactly one mole.
diagram.afterContent: The mass of the sample you have.

scenes.caption: Molar mass and mass are related, but they are not the same quantity.

voiceover.text: Do not confuse molar mass with mass. Molar mass is the mass of one mole. Mass is the amount of substance you actually have.

scenes.heading: Quick check
scenes.question: What is the mass of 0.50 mol of water, H₂O?
scenes.pausePrompt: Pause: find M first, then m.
scenes.caption: Find the molar mass of water, then multiply by 0.50 mol.

voiceover.text: What is the mass of zero point five zero mole of water? Find molar mass first, then multiply by moles.

scenes.heading: Summary
scenes.finalPrompt: Before calculating: do I need M or m?
points: Molar mass is the mass of one mole.
Molar mass is measured in g mol⁻¹.
Use m = n × M to calculate mass.
Always calculate molar mass from the chemical formula.
scenes.caption: Molar mass connects amount in moles to mass in grams.

voiceover.text: Molar mass is the mass of one mole. Use m equals n times M to calculate mass, and always calculate molar mass from the chemical formula.
